quotes.text: Digressions, incontestably, are the sunshine — they are the life, the soul of reading!
“The reader sees the world through the detective’s eyes, experiencing the proliferation of its details as if for the first time. He has become awake to the things around him.”
quotes.attr: The New York Trilogy by Paul Auster

quotes.text: “Trust nobody and love nothing except literature."
quotes.attr: Call Me Zebra by Azareen Van der Vliet Oloomi

quotes.text: “I have not died; for I always carry a little notebook and pencil to annotate everything, and if that had happened to me, I would have written it down.”
“It’s the opposite of a blog. It’s an antiblog, because it’s meant for only one special person, and that person is you.”
quotes.attr: A Tale for the Time Being by Ruth Ozeki

quotes.text: “Whether you’re a human being, an insect, a microbe, or a stone, this verse is true. All that you touch, You Change. All that you Change, Changes you.”
quotes.attr: Parable of the Sower by Octavia E. Butler

about.headline: A few notes on the writer.
about.deck: Books, the Great Depression, the card game euchre, AI & writing, and the curious corners of culture.
paragraphs: Jason Boog is a writer and editor in Los Angeles. He is the author of *Born Reading* (Touchstone) and *The Deep End: The Literary Scene in the Great Depression and Today*, and edits at [Fable](https://fable.co), where he works on tools for readers and writers.

He has reported on books, technology, and labor for outlets including the Los Angeles Times, Wired, Salon, and Publishers Weekly, where he edited GalleyCat. His current obsessions include WPA-era literary magazines, the patient strangeness of the card game euchre, and what it means to write alongside a machine that talks back.
He's also the proud parent of a small bookworm, a habit of three re-reads a year, and a long-running suspicion that the most interesting writing happens in the margins.
If a piece of his work has landed in your hands at the right moment, he'd love to hear about it. The [letters page](/contact.html) is always open.
